examples.explanation: Start with empty subsequence [], index=0, element=5. Include 5 → [5], index=1, element=2. Include 2 (2<5) → [5,2], index=2, element=8. Cannot include 8 (8>2), exclude → [5,2], index=3, element=6. Cannot include 6 (6>2), exclude → [5,2], index=4, element=3. Include 3 (3<2) → [5,2,3], index=5, element=6. Cannot include 6 (6>3), exclude → [5,2,3], index=6, element=9. Cannot include 9 (9>3), exclude → [5,2,3], index=7, element=5. Include 5 (5<3) → [5,2,3,5], length=4. Backtrack to explore other paths: from [5,2], exclude 3 → [5,2], include 6? no, include 9? no, include 5? (5<2) → [5,2,5], length=3. Another path: start with exclude 5 → [], include 2 → [2], include 8? no, include 6? no, include 3 (3<2) → [2,3], include 6? no, include 9? no, include 5 (5<3) → [2,3,5], length=3. Maximum found is 4 from [5,2,3,5].

Start with empty [], index=0, element=1. Include 1 → [1], index=1, element=2. Cannot include 2 (2>1), exclude → [1], index=2, element=3. Cannot include 3 (3>1), exclude → [1], index=3, element=4. Cannot include 4 (4>1), exclude → [1], length=1. Explore exclude paths: exclude 1 → [], include 2 → [2], include 3? no, include 4? no → length=1. Similarly for starting with 3 or 4. All subsequences of length >1 are not decreasing since array is increasing. Maximum is 1.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all subsequences recursively, checking each for decreasing order and tracking the longest. Use recursion to explore include/exclude decisions for each element.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Decreasing Subsequence via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution generating all subsequences to find the longest decreasing one.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Longest Decreasing Subsequence (LDS) problem by exploring all possible subsequences of the input array using recursion. The intuition is that for each element, we have two choices: include it in the current subsequence being built or exclude it. By recursively exploring both options, we generate all 2^n possible subsequences. For each generated subsequence, we check if it's strictly decreasing (each element less than the previous) and track the maximum length found. The recursion starts with an empty subsequence and processes elements from left to right. At each step, we can include the current element only if it maintains the decreasing property with the last element in the current subsequence (or if the subsequence is empty). The base case occurs when we've processed all elements, at which point we return the length of the current decreasing subsequence. The algorithm uses backtracking: after trying to include an element, we backtrack by removing it to explore the exclude option. Time complexity is O(2^n) because we generate all subsequences, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and current subsequence storage. This brute force method is foundational for understanding more optimized approaches like dynamic programming. Common mistakes include forgetting to check decreasing order before including elements, not properly backtracking, or incorrectly handling empty subsequences. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the recursive generation of subsequences with decreasing order validation.

examples.trace: Start with empty subsequence [], index=0, element=5. Include 5 → [5], index=1, element=2. Include 2 (2<5) → [5,2], index=2, element=8. Cannot include 8 (8>2), exclude → [5,2], index=3, element=6. Cannot include 6 (6>2), exclude → [5,2], index=4, element=3. Include 3 (3<2) → [5,2,3], index=5, element=6. Cannot include 6 (6>3), exclude → [5,2,3], index=6, element=9. Cannot include 9 (9>3), exclude → [5,2,3], index=7, element=5. Include 5 (5<3) → [5,2,3,5], length=4. Backtrack to explore other paths: from [5,2], exclude 3 → [5,2], include 6? no, include 9? no, include 5? (5<2) → [5,2,5], length=3. Another path: start with exclude 5 → [], include 2 → [2], include 8? no, include 6? no, include 3 (3<2) → [2,3], include 6? no, include 9? no, include 5 (5<3) → [2,3,5], length=3. Maximum found is 4 from [5,2,3,5].

Start with empty [], index=0, element=1. Include 1 → [1], index=1, element=2. Cannot include 2 (2>1), exclude → [1], index=2, element=3. Cannot include 3 (3>1), exclude → [1], index=3, element=4. Cannot include 4 (4>1), exclude → [1], length=1. Explore exclude paths: exclude 1 → [], include 2 → [2], include 3? no, include 4? no → length=1. Similarly for starting with 3 or 4. All subsequences of length >1 are not decreasing since array is increasing. Maximum is 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Generation of All Subsequences
ladders.desc: Generate all possible subsequences of an array using include/exclude recursion.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on generating all subsequences of a given array without yet considering the decreasing order constraint. The intuition is that a subsequence is formed by making a binary decision for each element: include it or exclude it. By recursively exploring both choices for every element, we can enumerate all 2^n possible subsequences. The algorithm starts at the first index and proceeds to the end. At each step, we first try including the current element in the current subsequence (by appending it to a list), then recurse to the next index. After returning from the include branch, we backtrack by removing the element and try excluding it (by simply recursing without appending). The base case is when we reach beyond the last index, at which point we have a complete subsequence that can be processed (e.g., printed or stored). Time complexity is O(2^n) since we generate 2^n subsequences, each taking O(n) time to build, but typically we consider the number of recursive calls. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and the current subsequence storage. This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach; in L0, we add the decreasing order check during the include decision. Common mistakes include not backtracking properly (failing to remove the element after the include branch), or incorrect base case handling. The connection to L0 is that this generation forms the skeleton, and L0 adds the decreasing condition.

examples.trace: Start: current=[], index=0, element=1. Include 1 → current=[1], recurse to index=1, element=2. Include 2 → current=[1,2], recurse to index=2 (base case) → output [1,2]. Backtrack: remove 2 → current=[1], exclude 2 → recurse with current=[1] to index=2 → output [1]. Backtrack to index=0: remove 1 → current=[], exclude 1 → recurse to index=1, element=2. Include 2 → current=[2], recurse to index=2 → output [2]. Exclude 2 → recurse with current=[] to index=2 → output []. Total outputs: [], [1], [2], [1,2].

Start: current=[], index=0, element=3. Include 3 → current=[3], recurse to index=1 (base case) → output [3]. Backtrack: remove 3 → current=[], exclude 3 → recurse to index=1 → output []. Total outputs: [], [3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking Decreasing Order in a Sequence
ladders.desc: Determine if a sequence is strictly decreasing and find its length.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves verifying that a given sequence (list of numbers) is strictly decreasing, meaning each element is less than the previous one. The intuition is to iterate through the sequence from the second element onward, comparing each element with its predecessor. If at any point the current element is greater than or equal to the previous, the sequence is not strictly decreasing. If the loop completes without such a violation, the sequence is decreasing. Additionally, we need to handle edge cases: an empty sequence has length 0 and is trivially decreasing (or undefined, but typically we consider it as a valid decreasing subsequence of length 0), and a single-element sequence is always decreasing with length 1. The algorithm is straightforward: initialize a variable to track the last element seen (starting with the first element), and for each subsequent element, check if it is less than the last element; if yes, update the last element and continue, else return false. Time complexity is O(k) for a sequence of length k, and space complexity is O(1). This logic is essential in L1 and L0 because when generating subsequences, we only include an element if it maintains the decreasing property. Common mistakes include using <= instead of < for strict decrease, or mishandling empty sequences. The connection to L1 is that this check is applied during the include decision in subsequence generation.

examples.trace: Sequence: [5,3,2,1]. Start with last=5. Check index 1: 3 < 5? yes, update last=3. Index 2: 2 < 3? yes, update last=2. Index 3: 1 < 2? yes. All checks pass, sequence is strictly decreasing. Length is 4.

Sequence: [4,4,3]. Start with last=4. Check index 1: 4 < 4? no (equal), so not strictly decreasing. Stop, return false. Length of decreasing subsequence is 0 since it's not decreasing, but if considering prefix, longest decreasing prefix is [4] of length 1, but overall sequence fails.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Backtracking with State
ladders.desc: Use recursion to explore choices while maintaining and updating state.
ladders.explanation: This building block introduces the concept of recursive backtracking, where we explore multiple possibilities by making choices, updating state, recursing, and then backtracking to revert state changes. The intuition is that many problems involve a sequence of decisions; backtracking allows us to try all combinations efficiently using recursion. Here, the state typically includes a data structure (like a list) that represents the current partial solution. At each recursive step, we have options (e.g., include or exclude an element). We choose one option, update the state accordingly, recurse to explore further, then backtrack by undoing the state change to try other options. The base case is when no more decisions are needed, at which point we process the complete state. This pattern is fundamental to generating permutations, combinations, or subsequences. Time complexity depends on the number of recursive calls, often exponential. Space complexity is O(depth of recursion) for the call stack plus state storage. In L2 and L1, this pattern is used to generate subsequences: state is the current subsequence list, choices are include/exclude, and backtracking involves removing an element after the include branch. Common mistakes include forgetting to backtrack, incorrectly sharing state between recursive calls (e.g., using mutable objects without copying), or infinite recursion due to missing base cases. The connection to L2 is that backtracking provides the framework to build sequences that can then be checked for decreasing order.

examples.input: Generate all binary strings of length 2
examples.trace: State: current string ''. Index=0, choices: add '0' or '1'. Choose '0' → state='0', recurse to index=1. At index=1, choose '0' → state='00', base case (length=2) → output '00'. Backtrack: remove last char → state='0', choose '1' → state='01', output '01'. Backtrack to index=0: remove '0' → state='', choose '1' → state='1', recurse to index=1. Choose '0' → state='10', output '10'. Choose '1' → state='11', output '11'. Done.

State: current set []. Index=0, element='a'. Include 'a' → state=['a'], recurse to index=1, element='b'. Include 'b' → state=['a','b'], base case → output {a,b}. Backtrack: remove 'b' → state=['a'], exclude 'b' → recurse with state=['a'] → output {a}. Backtrack to index=0: remove 'a' → state=[], exclude 'a' → recurse to index=1. Include 'b' → state=['b'], output {b}. Exclude 'b' → state=[], output {}. Done.

testCases.expected: HHH, HHT, HTH, HTT, THH, THT, TTH, TTT

testCases.input: All ways to choose 1 item from {A, B, C}
All sequences of length 2 from digits {0,1}
Generate all paths in a 2x2 grid (right/down moves)
testCases.expected: RRDD, RDRD, RDDR, DRRD, DRDR, DDRR

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Numbers in a List
ladders.desc: Iterate through a list and compare adjacent elements.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing a list of numbers and performing comparisons between consecutive elements. The intuition is that many algorithms, including checking for monotonic sequences, require examining pairs of adjacent items. The operation is performed using a loop that starts from the second element (index 1) and goes to the last element. In each iteration, we compare the current element with the previous one using relational operators (e.g., <, >, <=, >=). Based on the comparison, we might update a variable, return a result, or continue. For example, to check if a list is decreasing, we compare each element with its predecessor to ensure it is smaller. This operation is fundamental because it underpins the logic in L3 and L2 for validating sequences. Time complexity is O(n) for a list of length n, as we do one comparison per element (except the first). Space complexity is O(1) if we only use a few variables. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., starting loop at index 0 or going beyond the list length), incorrect comparison operators, or not handling empty or single-element lists. The connection to L3 is that this comparison is used within the backtracking framework to decide whether to include an element in a subsequence based on the decreasing condition.

examples.trace: List: [5,3,8,1]. Start loop at index 1: compare 3 and 5 → 3 < 5 is true. Index 2: compare 8 and 3 → 8 < 3 is false. Index 3: compare 1 and 8 → 1 < 8 is true. We can use these results to determine if the list is decreasing (it's not, due to 8>3).
List: [10]. Since there's only one element, there are no adjacent pairs to compare. In algorithms, we often handle this as a base case: if length <= 1, it's trivially decreasing (or increasing).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Subsequences
ladders.desc: Learn what a subsequence is and how it differs from subarrays or subsets.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces subsequences, a fundamental idea in computer science and algorithms. A subsequence of a sequence (like an array or string) is a new sequence formed by deleting zero or more elements from the original without changing the order of the remaining elements. Importantly, the elements in a subsequence maintain their relative order as in the original sequence. For example, given [1,2,3], possible subsequences include [], [1], [2], [3], [1,2], [1,3], [2,3], and [1,2,3]. Key points: subsequences are not necessarily contiguous (unlike subarrays, which are contiguous), and they are different from subsets because subsets are unordered collections, whereas subsequences preserve order. The intuition is that subsequences represent selections of elements where order matters, making them relevant for problems involving sequences, such as the Longest Decreasing Subsequence. The number of subsequences of a sequence of length n is 2^n, because for each element, you have two choices: include it or exclude it. This exponential growth is why brute-force approaches checking all subsequences have O(2^n) time complexity. Understanding this is crucial for L4 and above, as the brute force recursion generates all subsequences. Common mistakes include confusing subsequences with subarrays or permutations, or misunderstanding that elements can be skipped but order is fixed. The connection to L4 is that once we understand subsequences, we can iterate through lists to compare elements within them.

examples.trace: Start with empty subsequence []. For element A: include → [A], exclude → []. For B: from [A], include B → [A,B], exclude → [A]; from [], include B → [B], exclude → []. For C: from [A,B], include C → [A,B,C], exclude → [A,B]; from [A], include C → [A,C], exclude → [A]; from [B], include C → [B,C], exclude → [B]; from [], include C → [C], exclude → []. Collect all: 8 subsequences.
Empty: []. For X: include → [X], exclude → []. For Y: from [X], include Y → [X,Y], exclude → [X]; from [], include Y → [Y], exclude → []. Total: 4 subsequences.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Dynamic Programming O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use a DP array where dp[i] stores the length of the longest decreasing subsequence ending at index i. Iterate and compare with previous elements to update lengths.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Decreasing Subsequence with DP O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dynamic programming to find the longest decreasing subsequence in an array.
ladders.explanation: The longest decreasing subsequence (LDS) problem asks: given an array of integers, find the length of the longest subsequence where elements are strictly decreasing. A subsequence maintains order but can skip elements. The DP O(n²) approach solves this by building a DP array where dp[i] stores the length of the longest decreasing subsequence ending at index i. Intuitively, for each element nums[i], we look at all previous elements nums[j] (j < i). If nums[i] < nums[j], then nums[i] can extend the decreasing subsequence ending at nums[j], so dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). The answer is the maximum value in dp. This works because we consider all possible endings and build optimal solutions bottom-up. Time: O(n²) from nested loops. Space: O(n) for dp array. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of comparing each element with all previous ones to update dp. Common mistakes: forgetting to initialize dp to 1 (each element is a subsequence of length 1), using ≤ instead of < for strict decrease, or not tracking the maximum properly. Real code pattern: def longest_decreasing_subsequence(nums): n = len(nums); dp = [1] * n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if nums[i] < nums[j]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1); return max(dp).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare Each Element with All Previous Elements
ladders.desc: For each index i, iterate through all j < i to update DP values based on decreasing condition.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the DP O(n²) approach. For a given index i in the array, we examine all previous indices j (0 to i-1) to determine if nums[i] can extend a decreasing subsequence ending at nums[j]. The intuition: to find the best LDS ending at i, we need to consider all possible predecessors j where nums[i] < nums[j], and take the maximum dp[j] + 1. This ensures dp[i] is optimal. Step-by-step: Initialize dp array with 1s. For i from 1 to n-1: For j from 0 to i-1: If nums[i] < nums[j]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1). Time: O(n²) in worst case as inner loop runs i times for each i. Space: O(1) extra besides dp. Connection to L0: This subroutine is executed in the outer loop of the full solution. Common mistakes: starting i at 0 (no previous elements), incorrect loop bounds, or not using max to update dp[i]. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, n): for j in range(i): if nums[i] < nums[j]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1).

examples.trace: Given dp=[1,1,1,1] initially. i=3 (element 2). j=0: 2<3? yes, dp[3]=max(1,1+1)=2; j=1: 2<1? no; j=2: 2<4? yes, dp[3]=max(2,1+1)=2; final dp[3]=2.

Given dp=[1,2,3,4,1] after previous iterations. i=4 (element 1). j=0: 1<5? yes, dp[4]=max(1,1+1)=2; j=1: 1<4? yes, dp[4]=max(2,2+1)=3; j=2: 1<3? yes, dp[4]=max(3,3+1)=4; j=3: 1<2? yes, dp[4]=max(4,4+1)=5; final dp[4]=5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP State: Length of LDS Ending at Each Index
ladders.desc: Define dp[i] as the length of the longest decreasing subsequence ending exactly at index i.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is to define a DP state that captures optimal substructure. Let dp[i] be the length of the longest decreasing subsequence (LDS) that ends with the element at index i. This works because the LDS problem has optimal substructure: the best LDS ending at i can be built from the best LDS ending at some previous j where nums[i] < nums[j]. Intuitively, we break the problem into smaller subproblems (LDS ending at each position) and combine them. Step-by-step: Base case: dp[i] = 1 for all i (each element alone is a decreasing subsequence). Recurrence: For each i, dp[i] = max(1, max_{j < i and nums[i] < nums[j]} (dp[j] + 1)). The answer is max(dp). Time to compute one dp[i]: O(i) from checking all j. Space: O(n) for dp array. Connection to L1: This state definition is used in the subroutine to update dp[i]. Common mistakes: defining dp[i] as global LDS up to i (which doesn't work for recurrence), or not initializing to 1. Real code pattern: dp = [1] * n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): if nums[i] < nums[j]: dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through Array with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to compare each element with all previous elements in the array.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating through an array with an outer loop and an inner loop that goes from start to the outer index. The intuition: to process all pairs (i, j) where j < i, which is necessary for many DP problems including LDS. Step-by-step: For i from 0 to n-1: For j from 0 to i-1: Perform some operation comparing nums[i] and nums[j]. This ensures each element is compared with every element before it. Time: O(n²) as total iterations are sum_{i=0}^{n-1} i = n(n-1)/2. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This loop structure is used to apply the DP recurrence. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in loop ranges, or using j <= i which includes self-comparison. Real code pattern: for i in range(n): for j in range(i): # compare nums[i] and nums[j].

examples.trace: n=4; i=0: j loop empty; i=1: j=0: compare nums[1]=2 and nums[0]=1; i=2: j=0: compare 3 and 1; j=1: compare 3 and 2; i=3: j=0: compare 4 and 1; j=1: compare 4 and 2; j=2: compare 4 and 3; total 6 pairs.
n=3; i=0: no j; i=1: j=0: compare nums[1]=1 and nums[0]=5; i=2: j=0: compare 3 and 5; j=1: compare 3 and 1; total 3 pairs.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Two Numbers for Strict Decrease
ladders.desc: Check if one number is strictly less than another.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is a simple comparison: given two numbers a and b, check if a < b. In the context of LDS, we use this to determine if an element can extend a decreasing subsequence. Intuitively, for a subsequence to be decreasing, each new element must be strictly smaller than the previous one. Step-by-step: Input a, b; output True if a < b, else False. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is performed inside the nested loops. Common mistakes: using <= instead of < (which allows equal elements, making it non-strict decrease), or comparing in wrong order. Real code pattern: if a < b: # do something.

examples.trace: 3 < 5 is True, so output True.
7 < 7 is False, so output False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Subsequence?
ladders.desc: Understand that a subsequence is a sequence derived by deleting some elements without changing order.
ladders.explanation: A subsequence of an array is a sequence that can be derived by deleting zero or more elements from the original array, without changing the order of the remaining elements. Intuitively, you can skip elements, but you must maintain the original relative order. For example, from [1,2,3], subsequences include [1,2], [1,3], [2,3], [1,2,3], [1], [2], [3], and [] (empty). This is different from a substring or subarray, which must be contiguous. Step-by-step: Given array A of length n, a subsequence is defined by a set of indices i1 < i2 < ... < ik where k ≤ n, and the subsequence is [A[i1], A[i2], ..., A[ik]]. Time to list all subsequences: O(2^n) as there are 2^n possible subsets. Space depends on representation. Connection to L4: In LDS, we look for decreasing subsequences, so understanding subsequences is fundamental. Common mistakes: confusing with subarray (contiguous) or subset (order doesn't matter). Real code pattern: Often represented by indices or a new list.

examples.trace: Start with empty: []. Pick indices: {0} gives [1]; {1} gives [2]; {2} gives [3]; {0,1} gives [1,2]; {0,2} gives [1,3]; {1,2} gives [2,3]; {0,1,2} gives [1,2,3]. Total 8 subsequences.
Empty: []. Indices: {0} gives [5]; {1} gives [10]; {0,1} gives [5,10]. Total 4 subsequences.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Patience Sorting O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Maintain a list of piles where each pile represents a decreasing subsequence, using binary search to place elements. The number of piles gives the length of the longest decreasing subsequence.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Decreasing Subsequence using Patience Sorting
ladders.desc: Complete O(n log n) solution using patience sorting with binary search
ladders.explanation: The longest decreasing subsequence (LDS) problem asks: given an array of integers, find the length of the longest subsequence where elements are strictly decreasing. The patience sorting approach provides an O(n log n) solution by maintaining piles of decreasing subsequences. Intuition: Imagine sorting cards into piles where each pile's top card is decreasing from left to right. When processing a new element, we find the leftmost pile whose top is less than or equal to the element (using binary search) and place it there. If no such pile exists, start a new pile. The number of piles at the end equals the LDS length. Why this works: Each pile represents a decreasing subsequence, and the greedy placement ensures we maintain the smallest possible top values, allowing more elements to extend existing subsequences. The algorithm processes each element once, using binary search (O(log n)) to find the correct pile, giving O(n log n) time. Space is O(n) for storing pile tops. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty list 'piles'. 2. For each element x in array: a. Binary search in piles for the leftmost pile where top >= x (since we want decreasing, we look for piles where top is >= x to place x on top, maintaining decreasing order). b. If found, replace that pile's top with x. c. Else, append x as new pile. 3. Return length of piles. Connection to L1: This uses the binary search helper from L1 to efficiently find pile positions. Common mistakes: Confusing increasing vs decreasing (use >= for decreasing), not handling empty arrays, incorrect binary search bounds. Real code pattern: piles = []; for x in arr: idx = bisect_left(piles, x, key=lambda p: -p) (if using reverse logic) or implement custom binary search; if idx < len(piles): piles[idx] = x; else: piles.append(x); return len(piles).

examples.trace: Array: [10,9,2,5,3,7,101,18]. Process 10: piles=[] -> new pile -> piles=[10]. Process 9: binary search in piles for leftmost >=9 -> piles[0]=10 >=9 -> replace -> piles=[9]. Process 2: binary search for >=2 -> piles[0]=9 >=2 -> replace -> piles=[2]. Process 5: binary search for >=5 -> piles[0]=2 <5 -> not found -> new pile -> piles=[2,5]. Process 3: binary search for >=3 -> piles[0]=2 <3, piles[1]=5 >=3 -> replace piles[1] -> piles=[2,3]. Process 7: binary search for >=7 -> piles[0]=2<7, piles[1]=3<7 -> not found -> new pile -> piles=[2,3,7]. Process 101: binary search for >=101 -> all tops <101 -> new pile -> piles=[2,3,7,101]. Process 18: binary search for >=18 -> piles[0]=2<18, piles[1]=3<18, piles[2]=7<18, piles[3]=101>=18 -> replace piles[3] -> piles=[2,3,7,18]. Final piles length=4. LDS example: [10,9,5,3] or [10,9,7,18] etc.

Array: [0,1,0,3,2,3]. Process 0: piles=[] -> new pile -> piles=[0]. Process 1: binary search for >=1 -> piles[0]=0<1 -> not found -> new pile -> piles=[0,1]. Process 0: binary search for >=0 -> piles[0]=0>=0 -> replace -> piles=[0,1]. Process 3: binary search for >=3 -> piles[0]=0<3, piles[1]=1<3 -> not found -> new pile -> piles=[0,1,3]. Process 2: binary search for >=2 -> piles[0]=0<2, piles[1]=1<2, piles[2]=3>=2 -> replace piles[2] -> piles=[0,1,2]. Process 3: binary search for >=3 -> piles[0]=0<3, piles[1]=1<3, piles[2]=2<3 -> not found -> new pile -> piles=[0,1,2,3]. Final piles length=4? Wait, check: For decreasing subsequence, we need strictly decreasing. But here tops are increasing: 0,1,2,3. That's wrong because we're building increasing piles for LIS, not LDS. Correction: For LDS, we need piles where tops are decreasing from left to right. So binary search should find leftmost pile where top <= x? Let's redo with correct logic: For LDS, we maintain piles with decreasing tops. When processing x, find leftmost pile with top >= x (so x can go on top, making it smaller or equal, maintaining decreasing). Actually, for decreasing subsequence, each pile is decreasing, so new element x should be placed on leftmost pile where top >= x (so x <= top, keeping pile decreasing). Let's trace properly: Process 0: piles=[] -> new pile -> piles=[0]. Process 1: binary search for leftmost pile with top >=1 -> piles[0]=0<1 -> not found -> new pile -> piles=[0,1]. But now piles tops are 0,1 which is increasing, not decreasing. This is incorrect for LDS. The standard patience sorting for LIS uses increasing piles. For LDS, we can reverse the array and do LIS, or modify binary search. Better: Transform to LIS by negating numbers or reversing. Simpler: Do LIS on reversed array. Let's do LIS on reversed array: Reverse [0,1,0,3,2,3] -> [3,2,3,0,1,0]. LIS of reversed gives LDS of original. LIS patience: piles for reversed. Process 3: piles=[3]. Process 2: binary search for leftmost >=2 -> piles[0]=3>=2 -> replace -> piles=[2]. Process 3: binary search for >=3 -> piles[0]=2<3 -> new pile -> piles=[2,3]. Process 0: binary search for >=0 -> piles[0]=2>=0 -> replace -> piles=[0,3]. Process 1: binary search for >=1 -> piles[0]=0<1, piles[1]=3>=1 -> replace piles[1] -> piles=[0,1]. Process 0: binary search for >=0 -> piles[0]=0>=0 -> replace -> piles=[0,1]. Length=2. So LDS length=2. Example decreasing subsequence: [1,0] or [3,2]. So for L0, we implement LDS via LIS on reversed array. Final algorithm: reverse array, apply LIS patience sorting, return piles length. Time O(n log n), space O(n).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search in Patience Sorting
ladders.desc: Find position to place element in sorted list of pile tops
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the O(n log n) efficiency in patience sorting. Given a sorted list of pile tops (which are maintained in increasing order for LIS, or decreasing for LDS with modifications) and a new element x, we need to find the leftmost position where x can be placed such that the order is maintained. For LIS (increasing subsequence), pile tops are kept in increasing order. When processing x, we find the leftmost pile with top >= x (using binary search), and replace that top with x. If no such pile exists (x is greater than all tops), we start a new pile with x. Intuition: By placing x on the leftmost possible pile, we keep tops as small as possible, allowing future elements to extend subsequences. This greedy choice ensures the number of piles (LIS length) is correct. The binary search operates on the list of tops, which is always sorted, so each search is O(log n). Step-by-step: 1. Input: sorted list 'tops', element x. 2. Use binary search to find index 'idx' such that tops[idx] is the first element >= x. 3. If found, return idx; else return len(tops). Algorithm: left=0, right=len(tops); while left<right: mid=(left+right)//2; if tops[mid] < x: left=mid+1; else: right=mid; return left. Time: O(log n) where n is number of piles (at most array length). Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This function is called for each element in the array in L0. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in binary search bounds, not handling empty list, confusing < vs <=. Real code pattern: import bisect; idx = bisect.bisect_left(tops, x).

examples.trace: Binary search on sorted list [2,5,7] for leftmost >=4. Initialize left=0, right=3. Mid=(0+3)//2=1, tops[1]=5. Since 5 >=4, set right=1. Now left=0, right=1. Mid=(0+1)//2=0, tops[0]=2. Since 2 <4, set left=1. Now left=1, right=1, exit loop. Return left=1. So index 1 is where 4 should go (first element >=4 is 5 at index 1).

Binary search on [1,3,5] for leftmost >=6. Left=0, right=3. Mid=1, tops[1]=3 <6 -> left=2. Left=2, right=3. Mid=2, tops[2]=5 <6 -> left=3. Left=3, right=3, exit. Return 3, which is len(tops), indicating no element >=6, so x should start new pile.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Piles for Longest Increasing Subsequence
ladders.desc: Greedy pile maintenance strategy for LIS
ladders.explanation: This core logic explains how maintaining piles of cards (subsequences) leads to finding the LIS length. The insight: Instead of tracking all possible subsequences, we maintain a list of 'piles' where each pile's top card represents the smallest possible ending value for a subsequence of that length. As we process each element x from the array, we place x on the leftmost pile whose top is >= x (using binary search from L1). If no such pile exists, we start a new pile with x. The number of piles at the end equals the length of the LIS. Intuition: Each pile corresponds to a potential increasing subsequence of a certain length. By always placing x on the earliest possible pile, we keep the top values as small as possible, which maximizes the chance for future larger numbers to extend existing subsequences. This greedy approach works because it maintains the invariant that for any pile i, the top card is the minimum possible ending value for an increasing subsequence of length i+1. The algorithm processes elements in order, ensuring we consider all possibilities. Step-by-step: Initialize empty piles list. For each x: find index i via binary search where piles[i] is first >= x. If i < len(piles): piles[i] = x; else: piles.append(x). After all elements, return len(piles). Time: O(n log n) due to n elements each requiring O(log n) binary search. Space: O(n) for piles. Connection to L0: This is the main algorithm for LIS, which can be adapted for LDS by reversing the array. Common mistakes: Not understanding why piles length gives LIS, incorrect placement logic. Real code pattern: piles=[]; for x in arr: i=bisect_left(piles,x); if i==len(piles): piles.append(x); else: piles[i]=x; return len(piles).

examples.trace: Process 3: piles=[] -> new pile -> piles=[3]. Process 1: binary search in piles for leftmost >=1 -> piles[0]=3>=1 -> replace -> piles=[1]. Process 2: binary search for >=2 -> piles[0]=1<2 -> not found -> new pile -> piles=[1,2]. Process 4: binary search for >=4 -> piles[0]=1<4, piles[1]=2<4 -> not found -> new pile -> piles=[1,2,4]. Final piles length=3. LIS: [1,2,4].

Process 5: piles=[5]. Process 4: binary search for >=4 -> piles[0]=5>=4 -> replace -> piles=[4]. Process 3: binary search for >=3 -> piles[0]=4>=3 -> replace -> piles=[3]. Process 2: binary search for >=2 -> piles[0]=3>=2 -> replace -> piles=[2]. Process 1: binary search for >=1 -> piles[0]=2>=1 -> replace -> piles=[1]. Final length=1. LIS: any single element.

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding Subsequences vs Subarrays
ladders.explanation: This building block clarifies a fundamental concept: the difference between a subsequence and a subarray. A subarray is a contiguous sequence of elements from the original array, meaning you take elements in order without skipping any in between. For example, in array [1,2,3,4], [2,3] is a subarray. A subsequence, on the other hand, is a sequence that can be derived by deleting some elements (possibly none) without changing the order of the remaining elements. It does not need to be contiguous. For example, in [1,2,3,4], [1,3] is a subsequence but not a subarray because it skips 2. In the longest decreasing subsequence problem, we are looking for subsequences, not subarrays. This is crucial because it allows more flexibility: elements can be non-adjacent as long as they appear in decreasing order. Intuition: Since subsequences can skip elements, the problem becomes about selecting a subset of elements that maintain order, which is more complex than subarray problems (which can often be solved with sliding window). This distinction affects algorithm design: for subsequences, we often use dynamic programming or greedy approaches like patience sorting. Step-by-step to identify: Given array A, a subsequence is defined by indices i1 < i2 < ... < ik such that we take A[i1], A[i2], ..., A[ik]. A subarray requires indices to be consecutive. Time/space: Not applicable directly, but understanding this is key for problem analysis. Connection to L2: Patience sorting works for subsequences because it processes elements in order but allows skipping via pile placement. Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with subarray, leading to incorrect solutions like Kadane's algorithm. Real code pattern: No code, but conceptual: when problem says 'subsequence', think non-contiguous; 'subarray' means contiguous.

examples.trace: For subsequence: Take indices 0,1,3,4: values 10,9,5,3. This is decreasing and non-contiguous (skips index 2 with value 2). For subarray: Take indices 1,2,3: values 9,2,5. This is contiguous but not decreasing (2<5).
Subsequence: indices 0 and 2 -> values 1,3 (skips index 1). Subarray: indices 1,2,3 -> values 2,3,4 (contiguous).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through an Array
ladders.desc: Loop through each element of an array sequentially
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing an array from start to end, accessing each element one by one. It is fundamental to many algorithms, including patience sorting for LDS. Intuition: By iterating through the array, we can process each element in order, which is necessary for subsequence problems where order matters. Step-by-step: Given an array arr of length n, use a loop to go from index 0 to n-1. For each index i, access arr[i]. This can be done with a for loop or while loop. Time: O(n) since we visit each element once. Space: O(1) if no extra data structures are used within the loop. Connection to L3: Iteration allows us to consider each element for inclusion in a subsequence. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., going beyond array bounds), modifying array while iterating. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): x = arr[i]; or for x in arr: directly use x.

examples.trace: Initialize loop index i=0. Access arr[0]=5. Process 5. Increment i to 1. Access arr[1]=2. Process 2. i=2, arr[2]=8. Process 8. i=3, arr[3]=1. Process 1. i=4, end loop.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is an Array?
ladders.desc: Basic data structure: ordered collection of elements
ladders.explanation: An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements of the same type. It is ordered, meaning each element has an index (position) starting from 0. Intuition: Think of an array like a row of boxes, each holding a value, and you can access any box by its number. This allows efficient access to elements by index. Step-by-step: To create an array, define its size and type. For example, in Python, a list can be used as an array: arr = [1,2,3]. Access elements with arr[0] gives 1, arr[1] gives 2, etc. Arrays are used because they provide O(1) access time to any element if you know the index. Time/space: Accessing by index is O(1); storing n elements uses O(n) space. Connection to L4: Iteration works on arrays by using indices. Common mistakes: Accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., arr[5] for array of size 3), confusing with other data structures like sets. Real code pattern: arr = [10,20,30]; first_element = arr[0]; last_element = arr[-1].

examples.trace: Array has 3 elements. Position 0 (first) holds value 5. Position 1 holds 10. Position 2 holds 15. To access, use square brackets: arr[0] returns 5, arr[1] returns 10, arr[2] returns 15.
Array of strings. Element at index 0 is 'apple', at index 1 is 'banana', at index 2 is 'cherry'. Accessing arr[1] gives 'banana'.
